title: “We love our jobs!” Systems Thinking and Participatory Action Research for the future of neurodiversity and skilled employment
description: Although neurodivergent people can achieve high levels of academic and vocational training in the same professions as others, they remain un- and under-employed; this is in part due to the “wickedness” of the problem. To address the complexity we convened a group of neurodivergent lived experience and employment experts to  1) define which problems in neurodiversity and skilled employment are most urgent to address; 2) create actionable strategies with potential leverage for generating the desired employment outcomes. We used qualitative methods from the systems thinking field: Soft Systems Methodology, futures visioning and SWOT analysis, and Group Model Building (system dynamics). The workshops resulted in identifying intervention leverage between organizational psychology constructs of employee wellbeing, workplace climate, and mutually beneficial workplace relationships, in conjunction with a new disability stress construct, rather than specific skills building or remediation of disability characteristics. Reaching a future in which neurodivergent people love their jobs will take education, resources, champions from the outside and inside, universal access, paradigm shifts, infrastructure, and recognition of individual and social complexity. We recommend examining the organizational psychology literature for adaptable interventions; focusing on peer support; leveraging emerging technology to manage complexity; and supporting supervisor behavior change based on authentic understanding.

title: How to Implement Interdisciplinary Care in Persistent Pain: A Process-Based Framework for Clinical Decision-Making
description:
Interdisciplinary care is widely recognised as the gold standard for the management of persistent pain. However, despite strong theoretical support, its implementation remains inconsistent due to the lack of operational frameworks that guide how multidisciplinary information should be integrated into coordinated clinical decision-making. Existing biopsychosocial, process-based, and network-informed models provide valuable conceptual foundations but offer limited guidance on how clinicians should identify, prioritise, and target processes within interdisciplinary practice.
This paper builds on the previously proposed Process-Based Interdisciplinary Therapy (PbIT) framework by operationalising its application to interdisciplinary clinical decision-making in persistent pain. PbIT integrates the Pain and Movement Reasoning Model (PMRM), micro-level functional analysis, and the Extended Evolutionary Meta-Model (EEMM) into a recursive clinical decision-making system that supports shared formulation, process prioritisation, intervention selection, and ongoing treatment adaptation. Rather than prescribing discipline-specific interventions, the framework provides a flexible structure for coordinating professional expertise around modifiable biopsychosocial processes identified through interdisciplinary assessment.
To facilitate implementation, PbIT is operationalised through a six-step clinical workflow encompassing interdisciplinary assessment, network formulation, functional analysis, target prioritisation, intervention design, continuous monitoring, and readjustment. A hypothetical clinical case is used throughout to illustrate how the framework may support collaborative reasoning and adaptive treatment planning.
By making interdisciplinary clinical reasoning explicit, transparent, and reproducible, PbIT offers a practical framework for translating process-based and network-informed theory into coordinated interdisciplinary care. The framework has the potential to improve

title: Beyond Aggregate Accuracy: A Bayesian Time-Varying Autoregressive Model and Its Multilevel Extension
description: Autoregressive (AR) models are widely used to study within-person dynamics in intensive longitudinal data (ILD), but they assume constant parameters, an assumption that may not hold when the dynamics change, as during development, intervention, or recovery. The time-varying autoregressive (TV-AR) model accommodates such change by representing the intercept and AR coefficient as smooth functions of time. As originally proposed, however, it was developed for a single individual and evaluated with time-averaged accuracy metrics. We address these limitations by extending it to a Bayesian multilevel formulation with population-average and individual-specific time-varying parameters, and by emphasizing pointwise evaluation of accuracy rather than aggregate accuracy alone. Two simulation studies examined how well the single-level and multilevel models recover time-varying dynamics. Examining bias at each time point, we found that it was largest near the series boundaries and in regions of steep change or high curvature. The time-varying intercept and AR coefficient showed complementary biases that largely cancelled in the implied mean, which was therefore recovered with smaller bias. In the multilevel model, the best allocation between persons and time points depended on the parameter and inferential level: the AR coefficient favored longer series at both levels, whereas the implied mean favored more persons for population-average recovery but longer series for individual recovery. We discuss implications and recommendations for study design, result interpretation, and model selection.

title: Examining Linking Language and Causal Implications in Observational Psychological Capital Research
description: The present study examines whether the issues with implied causal inference identified by Haber et al. (2022) in observational health research extend to the field of industrial-organisational (IO) psychology. Haber et al. found that causal relationships were implied through the language used to link exposures and outcomes and through recommendations for action. This small-scale conceptual replication applies Haber et al.’s approach to psychological capital (PsyCap), an increasingly popular construct in the domain of IO psychology. Specifically, we evaluate the causal strength of the language used to describe the nature of the connection between given variables when reporting findings and when making recommendations for action in 50 PsyCap articles. The majority of action recommendations were found to have strong causal implications (85% in abstracts, 84% in discussion sections), and most linking sentences implied some level of causality (64% in abstracts, 58% in discussion sections). This was despite very few articles explicitly stating an intention to estimate causal effects (10%) and many explicitly warning against drawing causal inferences (42%). No significant relationship was found between the causality implied in the linking sentences and the strength of causal implication of the action recommendations. These findings have important implications for how research is implemented, and very real consequences for those who are the focus of such implementations. Increased transparency about causal interests and reasoning is needed to uphold the credibility of findings and the integrity of applications of observational PsyCap research.

title: A Cultural-Ecosocial Framework for Brain Health Research
description:
There has been enormous investment in neuroscience research to advance our knowledge of
neurological and psychiatric disorders and to identify the mechanisms that promote healthy
neurodevelopment and brain functioning. However, despite advances, much of this research has
focused on nonrepresentative populations, clinical samples, or laboratory analogues of real-world
settings. The application of neuroscience to brain health can be greatly enhanced by systematic
integration of contextual factors into research to ensure that findings are representative of the relevant
populations, and that they capture relevant variables of the environment and social-cultural context.
This contextual information should inform the design of studies, strengthen methodological rigour,
enrich the interpretation of results, and guide the translation of scientific findings into policy and
practice. The aim of this paper is to provide key principles for an integrative framework for brain health
research. This is motivated by the concern that to advance knowledge translation in clinical, public
health and policy domains, neuroscientific studies on brain disorders and mental health need to
incorporate measures of structural and social determinants of health in research to contribute to real-
world impact at scale.

title: What Makes a Habit? Investigating Potential Determinants of Habit Formation
description: Habitual behaviors are automatic responses to specific cues, acquired through repetition in stable contexts. While habits can support long-term behavior change, relatively little is known about which factors influence their development. We conducted a preregistered reanalysis of a six-month longitudinal study on habit formation during the COVID-19 pandemic. Participants (N = 254) completed monthly surveys assessing behavioral automaticity (i.e., habit) and ten potential determinants. Cluster analyses revealed two trajectories: habit formation and habit degradation, with cluster membership differing by behavioral complexity, frequency, and context stability. Multilevel models showed that six determinants were significantly associated with habit strength: autonomous motivation, behavior frequency, context stability, feeling energized, feeling pleased (positive), and perceived difficulty (negative). A significant interaction between time and behavior frequency emerged after correcting for multiple testing. Findings underscore the dynamic nature of habits and suggest that tailored interventions may be especially important during early formation and to prevent later degradation.

title: Model-based evidence for task-dependent representations in psychological similarity and working memory
description: A central question in visual working memory (VWM) is what psychological representation underlies performance. Standard measurement models assume recall error spreads over the physical stimulus space (e.g., a von Mises distribution on a color wheel), whereas the Target Confusability Competition model (Schurgin et al., 2020) proposes that error should instead be scaled by psychophysical similarity. Recent replications, however, find little benefit from similarity-based adjustments to VWM models (Oberauer, 2023; Tomić & Bays, 2024). One limitation of this debate is that similarity is often treated as a separate input—measured in an independent task or approximated parametrically—rather than inferred jointly with the cognitive processes that generate behavior. We address this limitation by developing Bayesian generative models that infer latent stimulus representations of oriented lines based on responses in perceptual reproduction, memory reproduction, and similarity comparison tasks (Tomić & Bays, 2024). The inferred representations deviate systematically from physical orientation, but in task-dependent ways: reproduction tasks show a bias towards the vertical meridian, whereas similarity judgments show attraction toward the cardinal orientations. Using Bayesian model comparison, we find that the task-specific representations provide a better explanation than a common-cause model that assumes a shared representation. This indicates mental representations should be modeled as part of task-specific generative processes, not assumed from similarity judgments alone. We conclude by highlighting two future research directions: attempting to unify our task-specific representations with additional cognitive processes, or attempting to catalog families of task-dependent representations by exploring different cognitive process models.

title: Principled Default Priors for Bayesian Hypothesis Testing in Binomial GLMs:   A Matched-Prior Framework with Empirical Evaluation
description: Researchers using Bayesian hypothesis testing in binomial generalized linear models (GLMs; for instance, logistic regression for accuracy data) need priors on regression coefficients, but no widely adopted default framework exists. The nonlinear link function creates a prior-link interaction: priors that appear reasonable on the coefficient scale can imply implausible distributions on the probability scale. We show that this problem has an exact solution for CDF-based link functions: matched priors – prior families whose CDF mirrors the link function – produce uniform distributions on the probability scale for the intercept, a maximally uninformative anchor. We extend this family choice to effect priors for parsimony, with prior scale determined via prior predictive simulation. We embed this matched-prior principle in a three-step workflow and evaluate it via simulation: (1) identify matched priors, (2) evaluate prior predictive distributions, (3) calibrate Bayes factors and compare to the JZS/Cauchy default. For logistic regression, the matched Logistic(0, 0.75) intercept prior and matched Logistic effect priors with scale 0.25 to 0.30 provide well-calibrated Bayes factors. A signal detection analysis shows that prior scale is a criterion parameter: it shifts the operating point on the ROC curve but does not change discriminability. The advantage of the matched-prior workflow is principled scale selection on the probability scale, where effect sizes are directly interpretable. The workflow extends naturally to hierarchical models, where the prior on the random-effects variance has negligible influence on Bayes factors for fixed effects. Ready-to-use brms code and scaling formulas for any contrast coding are provided.

title: Sociocultural Correlates of Infant Visual Attention
description: Infant visual attention—most often operationalized through looking behavior—has long served as a foundational method and explanatory construct in the study of early cognitive, perceptual, and learning processes. Despite extensive evidence that attention is plastic, experience-dependent, and embedded in structured environments, infant looking time is implicitly regarded as a culturally neutral measure. This review critically examines that assumption. Synthesizing evidence from cross-national, cross-linguistic, socioeconomic, and social-ecological research, infants’ visual attention varies systematically with culturally embedded experiences. Importantly, these differences do not map cleanly onto reified cultural categories (e.g. WEIRD/non-WEIRD), nor do they break down systematically by socioeconomic factors. Instead, the literature points to variability in experience-dependent tuning of distinct components of visual attention. Building on these findings, an acculturated model of infant visual attention is advanced in which endogenous attentional mechanisms interact dynamically with culturally structured experience. This approach strengthens the interpretive power and generalizability of attention-based methods in a continually diversifying developmental science.

title: Empathy shapes multimodal child-directed communication and children’s language comprehension
description: Effective communication requires speakers to tailor what they say, and how they say it, to their audience. Empathy may support this process, but it remains unclear whether empathic speakers adapt multimodal signals (speech, prosody, and gesture) in ways that benefit recipient understanding. We examined this question in child-directed communication. In Experiment 1, 46 broadcast-trained Chinese speakers produced semi-spontaneous programmes for child and adult audiences. Higher-empathy speakers showed stronger child-directed adaptation across lexical–syntactic, prosodic and gestural channels, including more audience-oriented lexical choices, enhanced pitch modulation, and more frequent and salient representational gestures. In Experiment 2, 221 children (7–11 years) viewed a subset of these materials from Experiment 1 for a comprehension task. Children understood child-directed programmes better when they were produced by higher-empathetic speakers, whereas empathy-related differences were minimal for adult-directed programmes. Cross-experimental mediation analyses showed that stimulus-level multimodal cues accounted for part of the association between speakers’ empathy and children’s comprehension. This indirect association was positive in child-directed programmes but negative in adult-directed programmes, indicating that empathy benefits children’s comprehension when speakers’ adaptations are aligned with children’s needs. These findings suggest that empathy operates as a higher-order communicative capacity, shaping audience-specific multimodal behaviour with consequences for children’s understanding.

title: Inference of light-source positions and of transparency in brightness illusion displays (Digital Typeset Edition)
description: Although many researchers have attempted to explain brightness illusions, no consensus agreement has yet been reached.  I here report that two factors are essential to the study of brightness illusions, and propose a Helmholtzian explanation for the illusions based on differences of logical interpretations for the displays.  The factors that there are: (1) a possibility that our visual system unconsciously infers three light-source positions for segments in the displays (the front of the segments, the segments themselves, and the area behind the segments) and (2) a possibility that our visual system interprets a non-transparent achromatic segment not only as a non-transparent surface illuminated from the side, but also as a transparent segment illuminated from behind, a shade, a cast shadow, a self-luminous segment, or a space.  If our visual system take the factors into account and selects the more likely interpretations out of possible interpretations, it is unsurprising that segments that are physically identical gray is perceived as a different brightness in each situation.

title: Working with Circular Data: A Tutorial for Cognitive and Behavioral Research
description: Introductory statistics courses and conventional methods in behavioral and cognitive science are typically restricted to Euclidean space without explicitly stating this, leaving many researchers unaware of how to handle data with circular or periodic properties (e.g., time of day or orientation on a screen). This tutorial provides an accessible yet rigorous introduction, designed to bridge the gap between statistical theory and practical application. After discussing different ways of representing circular data mathematically and fundamental principles for working with them, we explain angular and vector representations and operations, including key considerations for implementing them in computer code. We then survey methods for visualizing circular data, including linear and polar plots and presentation of summary statistics. Next, we introduce circular measures of central tendency, dispersion, and shape: how they relate to their Euclidean counterparts, and why they differ. Finally, we turn to more advanced topics of the kind needed to construct models of circular data. We introduce the most common circular distribution families, including circular analogs of the Gaussian distribution and their properties, as well as flexible distribution families that can describe skewed and heavy-tailed data. We survey common applications in the supplementary materials. The tutorial takes a method-oriented approach, not tied to specific software, but highlighting factors relevant to experimental design, data analysis, and statistical modeling. A consistent theme is the need to rigorously account for the inherent periodicity of circular data. We also provide a consistent notation for circular statistics and parameters, addressing the lack of established conventions in the field.

title: What is critical metascience and why is it important?
description: Metascience uses a scientific approach to understand and improve science. Critical metascience takes a step back to question metascience’s commonly-accepted assumptions, methods, problems, and solutions. In this article, I define critical metascience and explain how it can help to identify and address collective biases within metascience. I also highlight some emerging themes in the field. Finally, I consider some potential relationships between metascience and critical metascience and argue that the most functional model is one in which two fields are partially overlapping. I conclude that there needs to be greater recognition and dialogue between metascience and critical metascience in order to improve metascience’s objectivity and approach.

title: The Face of ChatGPT:  Using Reverse Correlation to Explore Mental Representations of AI
description: People readily attribute mental states to large language models, but it remains unclear what mental representation users hold of these systems when they personify them. Across two experiments, we used reverse correlation, a data-driven method originally developed in psychophysics, to visualize the perceptual content of users' mental representation of ChatGPT. In Phase 1, participants completed a reverse correlation task selecting which of two noisy faces looked most like ChatGPT were it human; in Phase 2, naïve participants rated the resulting classification images (CIs). Across both a female base image (Experiment 1) and a male base image (Experiment 2) image, ChatGPT CIs were perceived as warmer, more empathic, and more human-like than the base image, but did not differ reliably on competence or knowledge. By visualizing the human face people implicitly associate with ChatGPT, this study provides a novel window into the mental representations underlying contemporary Human-AI interaction. These representations may help explain how users come to trust, rely on, and socially engage with artificial agents.

title: Beyond search: solution-driven representation in human problem solving
description: Cognitive science has often characterized problem solving as search or planning within a well-defined representation. Recent accounts extend this view by treating representation construction itself as search over possible construals, guided by downstream plan utility or collisions during simulation. Yet when relevant features, actions, and constraints are unbounded, this bilevel search over representations and solutions may be computationally intractable. Drawing on field studies of naturalistic decision-making and design, we propose that people instead adopt solution-driven strategies: familiar candidate solutions are retrieved early and used to determine which problem features must be represented. Across three preregistered experiments spanning spatial navigation, abstract compositional reasoning, and cost-based itinerary planning, increasing representational demands increased reuse of familiar but suboptimal solutions. This reuse was not blind perseveration: participants selectively represented information needed to evaluate candidate solutions. Process tracing further showed that information search was centered on cached routes, yet less ordered along the route itself, consistent with holistic evaluation rather than biased incremental search. These findings suggest that solution-driven cognition is central to problem solving under large-world representational complexity.

title: Using a Master Narrative Framework to Examine Conversations about Race and Ethnicity
description:
Objective. Systemic racism, reinforced by color-evasive ideology, shapes the ways that emerging adults process and discuss race/ethnicity. In this preregistered and mixed methods study, we used a master narrative framework to examine narratives about race/ethnicity among U.S. emerging adult friend-pairs.
Method. Participants consisted of 107 friend-pairs (N = 214 total) who provided individually written racial/ethnic narratives and engaged in an unstructured conversation with each other in which race/ethnicity was brought up.
Results. Using quantitative methods, we found that meaning making of racial/ethnic experiences was higher in written relative to spoken narrative contexts. Additionally, using qualitative methods, we identified specific conversation dynamics capturing how young people discussed race/ethnicity including subthemes of mitigation and encouragement, finding that encouragement dynamics were related to higher meaning making.
Conclusion. Our discussion centers on the value of integrating qualitative and quantitative methods to explore how meaning of race/ethnicity is silenced or encouraged, and to reveal how master narratives perpetuating racial inequality may be disrupted.

title: From suspicion to violent action: A multi-category framework of fear speech using qualitative content analysis and machine learning
description: Current online hate speech flagging systems overlook more subtle forms of dangerous content that are not overtly violent, and more akin to what has been described as fear speech. Fear plays a crucial role in the process of radicalisation (Doosje et al., 2013), and is an important motivator of violence (Buyse, 2014). Yet, very little work has directly examined types and variations of online fear speech. In Study 1, we qualitatively content analysed a pre-annotated fear speech dataset to propose a novel framework of fear speech classification. This framework highlights a range of varying intensities of fear messages (Suspicion, Non-Violent Fear, Violent Fear, Non-Violent Call to Action, and Violent Call to Action). In Study 2, we generated synthetic data corresponding to our proposed framework, and used it to train a lightweight transformer-based model using contrastive learning, to enable automatic multilabel classification of fear speech. Our model outperformed multiple baselines, showing a decent ability to detect and classify fear speech. The proposed framework extends theorisation on hate speech and can be used to automatically detect less obvious harmful speech online, inform moderation policies and develop more appropriate counter fear-speech designs.

title: The trade-off between cooperation and intention-based fairness
description: Groups frequently ostracise members who are unable, rather than unwilling, to contribute to collective efforts, despite many people viewing such exclusions as fundamentally unfair. Across two experiments (N=520), we demonstrate that this apparent contradiction arises because, under ambiguity, when individuals cannot distinguish between intentional free-riding and genuine inability, groups face a dilemma: be tolerant and risk cooperation breaking down, or be intolerant and risk ostracising individuals who are unable to contribute and, arguably, deserve help. We show that depending on the economic costs of tolerance, groups adopt different strategies to address this dilemma. They either act in line with intention-based fairness ideals but risk cooperation decline or they adopt a more rigid stance and ostracize non-contributing group members, even when they know that these group members were unable to contribute. Our findings can elucidate why groups often act intolerantly towards vulnerable members, despite widespread norms endorsing intention-based fairness.
